howItWorks.intro: This seam allowance calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Cut Width = Finished Width + (2 × Seam Allowance)
Cut Height = Finished Height + (2 × Seam Allowance) + Hem Allowance
Fabric Length = (ceil(Pieces ÷ Pieces Across)) × Cut Height
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What is the standard seam allowance?
faq.answer: Commercial patterns: 5/8" (15mm). Quilting: 1/4". European patterns: 1 cm. Home sewing: 1/2" to 5/8". Specialty (lingerie, activewear): 1/4" to 3/8". Always check your pattern instructions.

faq.question: Why is 5/8" the standard seam allowance?
faq.answer: 5/8" provides enough fabric for finishing (serging, French seams, flat-fell), allows for fitting adjustments, and is easy to measure on most sewing machine throat plates.

faq.question: Should I add seam allowance to all edges?
faq.answer: Add seam allowance to all edges that will be sewn to another piece. Edges on a fold get no seam allowance on the fold line. Bottom hems usually get a separate, larger hem allowance (1-2").

faq.question: Is the Seam Allowance Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Seam Allowance Calculator?
sections.body: The Seam Allowance Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your finished width, finished height, seam allowance, you get instant results including cut width, cut height, fabric per piece. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting cut width right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your finished width and need to find the right cut width. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Finished Width (inches)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your finished width — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: finished height, seam allowance, number of pieces, hem allowance. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your cut width, cut height, fabric per piece. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your finished width before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both cut width and cut height — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your hem allowance, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
